speaker: interviewer
text: --22, 1995. Mr. Zelikson, you were telling us about the time when you were arrested because they thought you knew about some people who might be hiding.

speaker: interviewee
text: They took me in the-- in [? Dvinsk ?] jail. And then they interrogate me. The German, the Nazis, interrogate me there. And, like I said before, where they put the toothpicks in my fingernails, and burned, and electric shock, and the wall. But they didn't-- I didn't confirm anything. Because I didn't know anything. And then they put me in a place, which they built special for torture. You can't sit. You can't stay. And you can't lay down. It's-- you hold your hands like this, and in one that that you can't move. And from the top, water kept on dripping on my head. For the first hour, it didn't bother me. But after several hours, it's like a stone hits your head. And that was going on for about three, four days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you couldn't-- you had no room to move your hands or lift them.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I couldn't move my hand. I couldn't move nothing. It's built that one man goes in, in a certain position. And the head has to stand up tall. And the Nazis put the water on.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they do all these different things to you in one day? Was this one day?

speaker: interviewee
text: In one day, two days-- every day, they did something else, the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then they put you into this hole--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to try to see if you would--

speaker: interviewee
text: If I could tell them anything. But I didn't know anything. So I couldn't tell them anything, no matter what kind of torture they did to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any idea where your mother was, during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'm coming to that. Then, I was there what-- close to two months. And the Nazi guard says, you want to go up and clean the street, in the back? I said, yeah. I like to do work. Because I don't want to sit around. They're going to torture me again. And he was from the Wehrmacht. He wasn't from the SS. He says, clean the street. Do a good job. And if you do a good job, I'll let you do, by yourself, whatever you want to do, as long you promise you won't run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I'll promise you. And on the other side, I was cleaning the street, I saw my mother. A lot of women were there. They were cleaning the street. And the garden-- they had a garden. So I saw my mother. So I says to the German guard, this is my mother. Can I go over there? And he said yeah, go over there. Because he liked me. I listened to him. I did everything he told me. I went over to my mother. And then I says to him, can you do me a favor? I want to work with my mother, together. He says, it's not allowed, but I-- I'll see what I can do. And he came back in 15 minutes. And he says, OK. You're a good boy. You could work with your mother. Stay with your mother. But clean the street. Don't do anything else. Were you working with other people when you were doing these jobs? Or were you working--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were working with other people, who were working under this one guard? He was the guard--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, there were-- it must have been at least 30 or 40 guards. It was-- [? Dvinsk ?] jail is very big. They had maybe 2,000 prisoners there, or maybe more. And the guards was all over. But each person they assigned to a guard. And the guard was taking in charge of it. He was a sergeant-- [NON-ENGLISH]. That sounded like a [NON-ENGLISH]. And he told you what to do, how to do it. And you had to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you work for the same man, day in and day out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. For a while. For a while. I worked for about a month with them. And then I says to my mother, now we have a chance to escape. And we looked around. And nobody was there. He trusted us. And we escaped.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: We run away.

speaker: interviewer
text: There was nobody watching? You able just to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, [INAUDIBLE]. Where we were cleaning the street was right by the door to go outside the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Outside of the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: To leave the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. First we were in the park. And he told us to clean all around. Because he trusted us. So but-- by the gate-- the gate was open. So we just took a walk. Because the other guards thought whether we are cleaning from the outside the street.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was the gate unlocked?

speaker: interviewee
text: The gate was open. Because when the-- the people were cleaning the street, they let them out to clean all over.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you street cleaning the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The guard-- the guard was walking around. But where we-- I was, that was the only guard for my side. Because on the other side, on the right, behind the building, there was a lot of guard. But we were [? trusties. ?] So they weren't afraid of us. So they let us. So we walked away. And we walked away, and we got caught again. And this time, they took us to [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto. And they brought in, from a lot of other towns, a lot of Jewish people. And they came with trucks. And they took us with trucks. And then they put us in a freight train. And we heard--

speaker: interviewer
text: Just for a second-- I just want to understand something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: You escaped. Did you get away for a while? Did they catch you right away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- maybe in two hours.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think they were out looking for you, that they knew you were gone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the prison had sent people out to kind of catch you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But the-- the German civilian people caught us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what did they do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They called the [NON-ENGLISH] police-- the [NON-ENGLISH]. And the [NON-ENGLISH] took us back to [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: But they didn't return you to the jail?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even though you had escaped?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. They say, let them take to the [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto. Because they're going to be shot. We're supposed to be-- I'm sorry. We're supposed to get shot in Pogulanka. That was six o'clock in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait-- first, we're back at the [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto. Just let me understand.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're at the [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto. You've been returned there. What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: The next-- at-- at night, they brought in trucks-- a lot of trucks. And they put everybody in the trucks. And they brought us, with the trucks, to a freight train. And they put all of us in a freight train. My-- me and my mother was in one freight train. And the other people was in another freight. And they put us in like cows. And the people said, we're going to Pogulanka. They-- they're going to shoot us. When we were riding the train, the train slowed down by the forest, when we reached-- because Pogulanka, from [? Dvinsk, ?] is about 25 miles-- something like that. I don't remember exactly. And there's a big curve. And by the curve, the train slowed down. My mother-- there was a little window on the train, in the freight train. My mother says, we're going to run out from there. And she pushed me from the window. And after that, she went-- came out, through the window. And then, we saw the train stopped. And a lot of Jewish people-- I would estimate-- at that time, I was small. I couldn't count-- but I would estimate around 8,000 Jewish people. And they told them to take the clothes off.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now were you in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: We was in the forest, about five minutes away, where that happen. When we escaped from the train, we ran in the forest. And then we heard all the commotion-- the noise. So we wanted to see. My mother says, let's take a look what goes on. Then we saw the people came out from the freight train. And there was a lot of Nazis-- German-- and a lot of [? Latvians-- ?] [NON-ENGLISH]. And they all hold the rifles. And we saw about four or five trucks there. And they told the people to take off their clothes. And they didn't see yet the graves. So everybody took off their clothes. Because if you didn't take off the clothes, they-- they went with a whip and hit them. And then we inched over a little closer to the forest. We saw four graves-- gigantic graves. And they put the people right by the graves. And they told them to stay all on one side, by the graves. And then we saw the trucks open the canvas. And in the-- the trucks had, again, the big machine guns. And-- and they started to shoot the people. And the people were falling in the graves. But then the [? Latvians, ?] the [NON-ENGLISH], took the shovels, and started to shovel the earth over the people. And they kept on moving. So the German officer says, the devils. They're still not dead. Take-- everybody take the bayonet. And wherever it moves, stick the bayonet. But they kept on moving. Then he says, again, the officer, the Nazi-- he says, even if you want to kill them, the devils, you can't kill them. And then they took big sacks of white sand. To me, I didn't know what it was. Because I was, at that time, small. But my mother says, this is lime. And after they put, in a half an hour, the graves stopped moving. And then they were drinking and laughing. And he says, finally we killed the devil.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you and your mother do now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, I started to walk away. I wanted to relieve myself. And I started to walk. And in the forest, I didn't realize I walked that far. And I start to look for my mother. I couldn't find my mother. I kept on walking. Instead to walk to the right direction, I walked in the left direction. And I started to look-- find my mother. And I couldn't find her. And I cried. I looked all over. I couldn't find. I stood by a big tree. And the big tree opened up. And somebody grabbed me with my hand, and pulled me in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember a little bit-- when you say the tree opened up, do you think it was a door? Did they have-- do you remember at all what that looked like, how they hid that tree?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I think branches. They-- branches. As it opened up, I heard the squeaking, branches. It opened up. And I went in. There were six people. One, his name-- and I'll never forget-- [? Rigasofski. ?] And there was, next to me, a German sitting on a-- a tree trunk, a stump. He was sitting. And his hands was tied. And [? Rigasofski ?] takes out the gun. He says, shoot him. Because if you want to be with us, you have to kill him.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they say to you when they took you inside of this tree?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- first, they says, you want to stay with us? We're the underground. I says, sure. I want to stay with you. Because I'll be safe. He says, not that fast. He says-- and he showed me the German. He says, kill him. I threw away the gun. And I says, it's not in my religion. I don't know how to hold a gun. I have no knowledge. And I can't kill nobody. It's not in my religion. He took again the gun, put it in my hand, and he took out another gun, and put it to my head. He says, if you want to be with us, you have to kill him. And if you don't, I'll shoot you. Of course, I had no choice. And I-- he showed me how to do it. I didn't even know how to do it. He says, just slowly pull the trigger. And, all of the sudden, I saw the blood from his face squirted right in my face-- the whole blood. And I was sick. But I had to do it. And for years and years, I see, in my dream, the same-- for the-- I-- I always ask-- a lot of times, my wife wakes me up. Because she says I start to scream, shoot, shoot, shoot! And I did it. And then he says to me, now I'll teach you how to real do it, when you catch a German. He gave me a piece wire. I didn't even know-- it was piano wire. Later, they explained to me. And it had two loops, one for the-- one middle finger, and one for the other middle finger. And he says, you go behind him. And you just take the wire, and give it-- like this. And he'll be dead. Because they didn't want it we should carry knives or guns. The German Gestapo-- if they see somebody, they search you right away. And if they find it, they kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your mother with you at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had lost her in the forest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I lost her in the forest. Then I was in the underground, maybe a little over two months. And I went to get a German officer. I brought it in the underground, for interrogation. And they interrogate him. And I went out again. But I had to go in a different forest. And I got lost. I didn't know where to go back. And the only thing I thought about it-- that nobody's going to find me-- I'll go on a cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: The underground sent you, as a 12-year-old child, to go interrogate a German officer?

speaker: interviewee
text: To get-- to catch.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go catch him.

speaker: interviewee
text: To catch him--

speaker: interviewer
text: And bring him to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were going to-- [? Rigasofski ?] was going to interrogate him. But I had to go to catch.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you going to do that? Did you have a plan?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told-- they told me what they do. They told me. They said-- they-- they told me that a little-- a child, they won't suspect nothing. And when I caught him, he was sitting and drinking wine. I went with the loop, and put it on his neck, and he went with me. He had no choice.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so then you were saying you got lost?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got lost. Because they sent me to another forest. And I got lost. And I didn't know where to go. And the cemetery, the Dvinsk [NON-ENGLISH], is not too far from the cemet-- I mean, from the forest. So I went in the cemetery. And I didn't know where to sleep. So I-- there was trees by the graves. And I lay down. And I slept. And at night, the wind blows and makes-- oooh. And the branches move. You think the dead people are dancing. I was there for around four days. And I didn't have nothing to eat. And I could-- I was afraid to go to the farms to get something to eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see anyone else, during this time? Or you were completely alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, completely alone. And I started to dig the grave. I figured maybe I'll find something to eat. I dug out-- I dug deep. And I find a bone. Oh, I say, that's good. And I start to eat. At that time, I didn't even think it was a dead human being. But the bone was very hard. But I chewed. And that kept me a little from the hunger.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never had taken that soldier back? You just gotten lost on the way. And you got stuck in the cemetery.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I brought back the soldier. They send me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Another one.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another one to go. That's right. Because the forest was very big. And they told me that I did a good job and I should go to get another soldier. But it-- I [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And maybe [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two or three kilometer. And when I wanted to go back, I couldn't go back. I got lost.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't find it. Because in Europe, the forest is very thick. And when I saw the animals-- wolves-- I started to run. I saw that there were a lot of wolves. And that's how I wound up in the cemetery. And after I was on the cemetery, for about two months, somebody must have saw me-- something. The German Nazis started to look in the cemetery.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were in the cemetery, what did you do? How did you spend your day? What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I used to sleep during the day, hiding under the branches-- take a lot of branches, cover myself, and sleep. At night, I used to go look for food. But I couldn't find it. Because sometimes, in the forest, I-- in the farmer, I got a carrot, a raw potato. I ate. But during the day, I used to hide myself-- put a lot of grass on it. They had very big grass, a lot of small branches-- trees. And I hide myself. Because nobody was going on the cemetery. The Christians were afraid for the Jewish cemetery. They was very superstitious in Latvia. They thought the devil is going to catch them. And the Germans-- they were also afraid to go in  the cemetery. Well, somebody must have told them. So they went in the cemetery. And I run-- I run away. And I got caught. And they brought me again to [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto. There, I met my mother. My mother says she got lost. And a farmer took her to the house. And then she called the-- the [? Latvian ?] police. And the [? Latvian ?] police took her to [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto. And we came in [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto. And there was a German officer, a Nazi-- fell in love with a Jewish girl. And he begged her to-- she should change to German. And she says, no, she's Jewish. And she didn't want to go with him. And he took the revolver. And he shot himself. So the Nazis, the SS, the officer, he says, now I'm going to take 10 Jewish people. And I'm going to hang them. Because it's their fault the officer shot himself. And they called everybody out. And they told us to stay at attention. And then I saw the-- one of the German-- the kids. They take a ruler. And they take the measurement, the height. I was a child. But I didn't know why I did it. Till today I don't know. What gives me-- but sometimes maybe god works in mysterious ways. And I was small. So I says, I'm going to take off my shoes and step on top of my shoe, which makes me a little bigger. And he was going on the side, so he couldn't see it. And he says, [NON-ENGLISH]-- I should go to the right. My mother and my brothers-- to the left. So I says, I want to go with my mother and my brothers. And he took the rifle, with the butt, and hit me over the head. And he says, you devil. You go where I tell you to go, not where you want to go. And I no choice. He put me to the right. And then I saw buses. It was about three buses. And they put my mother and my brothers in the buses. And they told them that they're going to bring them in another camp-- a [NON-ENGLISH], they called it. And the people in the [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto told me, the buses has gas. And while they were riding, they gassed them. And the 10 Jewish people they took-- they hung them right in front of us. And they put a sign, that they killed the German officer. That's why they hang them. So everybody had to come out, to stand still, to attention, to watch the hanging. And then they took us, the remainder, from the ghetto, with trucks. And they told us they sending us to Kaiserwald. That was in Riga. Riga from Kaiserwald is about to 250 kilometers. And that was a concentration camp. We had to work very hard in the farm, and make streets-- build streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get from the [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto to Kaiserwald?

speaker: interviewee
text: They took us with trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went in trucks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there a lot of people? Were you in-- was it a whole--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --a bunch of trucks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Oh, must have been about 10 trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anybody you knew, at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, some of them I knew-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how were you in the trucks? Were you sitting, standing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they throw us in like-- like cows. They pushed us right in. They didn't give us sitting, or standing-- a lot of them, they were sitting on top of each other.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was that trip, from the [? Dvinsk ?] ghetto to the Kaiserwald? Do you remember? Days? Nights?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- I would say at least a night, I would say.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you arrived at the ghetto-- at the concentration camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember that, when you arrived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They took everybody out. And in Kaiserwald already, there was Jewish people assigned to guard us. The German, the Nazis, made them-- they had to put a star on-- by the breast-- a big star. And an armband-- and an armband it said [NON-ENGLISH]. They were in charge of us. And they were worse than the Nazis-- the Jewish [NON-ENGLISH]. They used to rape children. They raped women. And they did anything what they wanted to do. Of course, later they got killed, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that good?
